prompt: Share an untranslatable word or idiom from any language you know about — something that doesn't have a clean English equivalent — and explain what it captures.
response:
Word: “koi no yokan” (Japanese)

Literal pieces: koi = “romantic love”; no = possessive; yokan = “premonition/foreboding.”  
What it actually names: the precise, calm certainty you feel the first time you meet someone that the two of you are going to fall in love—even though you are not in love yet and may not even like each other very much at that moment.

English can say “I have a feeling we’ll end up together,” but that sounds like hopeful guesswork.  
Koi no yokan is not a guess; it is an almost physical hunch, neither wishful nor anxious, that the emotional script has already been written and you have just started reading it.
